  2. Week 2 — Core dplyr verbs

Week 2 — Core dplyr verbs

select, filter, mutate, arrange, summarize, group_by

dplyr
tidyverse
week-2
Published

April 30, 2026

Concept

dplyr gives you a small set of “verbs” that each do one thing to a data frame and return a data frame. The point is that any data manipulation, no matter how complicated, decomposes into a chain of these verbs piped together with |> (or %>%). That’s the whole grammar.

The six core verbs:

Verb What it does SAS analog
select() Pick columns keep / drop in a data step; select in proc sql
filter() Pick rows by condition where clause; if statement
arrange() Sort rows proc sort
mutate() Add or change columns assignment in a data step
summarize() Collapse rows to summary stats proc means / proc summary
group_by() Set the grouping for later verbs by statement

Why it matters

In SAS, “manipulating data” usually means writing a data step or a proc sql. The mental model is procedural: read each row, do something, output. In dplyr, the model is transformational: take a table, return a table. Once you internalize that, complex pipelines stop feeling like programming and start feeling like writing.

For ADaM work specifically, this matters because most ADaM derivations are exactly this shape — take SDTM, filter, join, derive, summarize. admiral is built on dplyr.

Code: a worked example

library(dplyr)

# A tiny PK-shaped dataset
adpc <- tibble::tribble(
  ~USUBJID,    ~AVISIT,    ~ATPT,     ~AVAL,
  "001",       "Cycle 1",  "Pre-dose", 0.0,
  "001",       "Cycle 1",  "1 hr",     12.4,
  "001",       "Cycle 1",  "4 hr",     8.1,
  "002",       "Cycle 1",  "Pre-dose", 0.0,
  "002",       "Cycle 1",  "1 hr",     14.7,
  "002",       "Cycle 1",  "4 hr",     9.3
)

# Cmax per subject per visit
adpc |>
  filter(ATPT != "Pre-dose") |>
  group_by(USUBJID, AVISIT) |>
  summarize(CMAX = max(AVAL), .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(USUBJID, AVISIT)

The pipe |> reads left-to-right: take adpc, then drop pre-dose rows, then group by subject and visit, then collapse to the max, then sort. Each step is a verb. The whole pipeline is one expression.

Resources used

  • R for Data Science (2e), Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel, Grolemund — Chapters 3–5 (free online). The canonical introduction.
  • dplyr cheatsheet from Posit (PDF). Print it. Tape it up.
  • vignette("dplyr") — the package’s own intro, runs in the console.

My summary — the things I want to remember

  • Pipes pass the left side as the first argument. x |> f(y) is f(x, y). Almost all dplyr verbs take the data frame as the first argument because of the pipe.
  • group_by() is sticky. Once a tibble is grouped, every subsequent verb respects the grouping until you ungroup() or use .groups = "drop" in summarize(). Forgetting this causes hours of confused debugging.
  • summarize() collapses, mutate() doesn’t. If you want a per-group statistic added back as a column without collapsing, use mutate() after group_by(), not summarize().
  • select() can rename in place: select(adpc, subject = USUBJID). Faster than calling rename() separately.
  • filter() drops NA silently when the condition involves an NA-producing comparison. If you want to keep NAs, use filter(is.na(x) | x > 0).
  • The .by argument in dplyr 1.1+ lets you do per-call grouping without group_by() + ungroup(): summarize(adpc, CMAX = max(AVAL), .by = c(USUBJID, AVISIT)). Cleaner for one-off aggregations.

Gotchas / things to remember for coding in R

Warning

R is case-sensitive. Filter is not filter. USUBJID is not usubjid. SAS forgives case in variable names by default — R never does.

  • dplyr::filter() is masked by stats::filter() (a time-series function loaded by default). If you ever get bizarre errors from filter(), prefix it as dplyr::filter() to be sure.
  • summarise() and summarize() are both valid — Wickham gave us both spellings. Pick one and stick with it.
  • n() only works inside dplyr verbs. It’s not a base R function. nrow(x) is the equivalent for the whole tibble.
  • Strings: dplyr uses bare column names (NSE), but values are quoted strings. filter(adpc, AVISIT == "Cycle 1") — the column is bare, the comparison value is in quotes.
  • == for equality, not =. = is assignment-in-an-argument and will not error the way you’d hope; it’ll just behave weirdly.
  • The pipe |> is base R (4.1+); %>% is from magrittr/dplyr. They’re almost identical but not quite. For new code, prefer |>.

Status

In progress. Next: pivots (pivot_longer/pivot_wider) and joins.
